---
title: "Saliency Maps"
author: Kris Sankaran
date: 2026-03-23
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    fig-align: center
execute:
    echo: true
    message: false
    warning: false
    cache: false
    eval: true
---

::: {style="display: none;"}
$$
\newcommand{\bs}[1]{\mathbf{#1}}
\newcommand{\reals}{\mathbb{R}}
\newcommand{\widebar}[1]{\overline{#1}}
\newcommand{\E}{\mathbb{E}}
\newcommand{\indic}[1]{\mathbb{1}\left\{{#1}\right\}}
\newcommand{\Earg}[1]{\mathbb{E}\left[{#1}\right]}
\newcommand{\Esubarg}[2]{\mathbb{E}_{#1}\left[{#2}\right]}
$$
:::

<style>
.purple { color: #7458d1ff; } /* pastel purple */
.orange { color: #fca020; } /* pastel orange */
.green { color: #3bbe67ff; } /* pastel green */
.darkblue { color: #4a9ceaff; } /* pastel dark blue */
.pink { color: #ee6ec3ff; } /* pastel pink */
</style>

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
library(tidyverse)
library(reticulate)
theme_set(theme_classic() + theme(panel.border= element_rect(fill = NA, linewidth = .5)))
set.seed(2026)
```

```{r}
#| label: python-setup
#| echo: false
# includes the SHAP package. Can install it using,
# > conda env create -f stat479_week6.yml
# where the yaml file is located at: https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat479_notes/blob/master/notes/stat479_week11.yml
use_condaenv("stat479_week12")
```

_Readings: [1]()_, _[Code](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat479_notes/blob/master/notes/12-transformers_handout.qmd)_

Items marked $^{\dagger}$ are not in the required reading and will not be
tested. The required reading covers some methods beyond this handout, those also
won't be tested.

## Setup

1. **Definition**. We view as single sample as a sequence of $N$ tokens, $x_{n}
\in \reals^{D}$. For example,

  - In language, we represent a document as a sequence of $N$ words. We could
  one-hot encode each word into a vector $e\left(\text{word}\right) \in \reals^{V}$ of length equal to the number of terms $V$ in the vocabulary. But
  to keep the dimensionality $D$ of the tokens manageable, we will usually apply
  an initial linear transformation $x_{n}\left(\text{word}\right) = W e\left(\text{word}\right)$ where $W \in \reals^{D \times V}$ and $D \ll V$.

  - In vision, we represent an image as a sequence of $N$ image patches. Here $D = W^{\text{patch}} \times H^{\text{patch}}$, the total number of pixels in each patch.

1. **Goal**. Transform a sequence of $N$ $D$-dimensional tokens into a
representation useful for downstream prediction tasks. Specifically, we want a
map parameterized by weights $\theta_{m}$,
$f_{\theta_{m}}: \reals^{D \times N} \to \reals^{D \times N}$,
\begin{align*}
  X^{m} = f_{\theta_{m}}(X^{m - 1})
\end{align*}
that can be applied recursively within a deep model, similarly to the ``linear
layer + nonlinear activation'' block used in MLPs.

1. **Requirements**

  - We must process the entire sequence at once. This is fundamentally different
  from MLPs, which could only process one vector at a time.

  - We must capture long-range dependence between tokens that are far apart in
  the sequence. It must ``remember'' information from earlier in the sequence.
  This is different from statistical models like GPs or traditional markov
  processes, which quickly ``forget'' earlier parts of the sequence.

  ![To see why long-range dependence is important, consider patterns like quotes in books or braces in code. These affect the interpretation of the text for a long time after they start.](figures/karpathy_sequences.png)

1. **Approach**. We will compose ``transformer'' blocks $f_{\theta_{m}}$ across
multiple $m$ to gradually learn more and more meaningful features of our input
sequence $X^{0}$,
\begin{align*}
X^{0} \xrightarrow{f_{\theta_{1}}} X^{1} \xrightarrow{f_{\theta_{2}}} \dots \xrightarrow{f_{\theta_{M}}} X^{M}
\end{align*}
Like in MLPs, the parameters $\theta_{1}, \dots, \theta_{M}$ will be optimized
to accomplish some downstream task. We omit details of this optimization, since
the training objectives are problem-specific.

1. **Preview**. Each block $f_{\theta_{m}}$ will have the form,

  (figure from paper)

  We've seen MLP and LayerNorm steps before, though we'll consider why they are
  helpful in this context. A critical, new component is the multihead
  self-attention operation (MHSA). This is what allows the transformation to
  learn long-range dependence.

  Everything below the first $\plus$ is called ``Stage 1'' of the transformer
  block and everything above is called ``Stage 2'''

## Stage 1: Attention across sequence

1. **Attention**. In layer $m$, we want a nonnegative matrix $A^{m} \in
\reals^{N \times N}$ whose columns sum to one. Column $n$ is viewed as a
distribution over input tokens $n'$ that are relevant to token $n$. Informally,
token $n$ ``attends to'' token $n'$ according to the weight $A_{nn'}^{m}$.

(picture of attention weights across a sequence)

1. If we knew $A^{m}$, we could compute,

\begin{align*}
Y^{m} = X^{m - 1}A^{m}
\end{align*}

and call it the output of stage 1. This sets the $n^{th}$ column of $Y^{m}$ to
be a mixture of columns of $X^{m - 1}$ where the mixing weights come from
$A^{m}$. There are unfortunately two problems,

   - Where do the attention weights come from?
   - This is not flexible enough to learn more meaningful representations.

  We'll resolve both issues step-by-step. To reduce the notational clutter,
  we'll suppress the $m$ superscripts for now -- the remainder of the note is
  focusing on a single layer $m$.

1. **MHSA (attempt 1: position-only)**. A first attempt would be to define
similarity according to the distance between tokens. For example,

  \begin{align*}
  A_{nn'} &= \frac{\exp{\left|n - n'\right|}}{\sum_{n' = 1}^{N} \exp{\left|n - n'\right|}}
  \end{align*}

  But this has no chance of learning long-range dependence, since it only
  attends to nearby tokens.

1. **MHSA (attempt 2: angle-based similarity)**. We can use the tokens
themselves to define attention^[This is why it is called ``self-attention.''].
A simple approach is to use the angle between the $x_{n}$ and $x_{n}'$ vectors:
we will attend more strongly to tokens that are closely aligned.
  \begin{align*}
  A_{nn'} &= \frac{\exp{x_{n}^\top x_{n^{\prime}}}}{\sum_{n' = 1}^{N} \exp{x_{n}^\top x_{n^{\prime}}}}
  \end{align*}
  Intuitively, by layer $m$, we hope that tokens $x_{n}, x_{n'}$ with similar
  ``meanings'' will already have representations that are in similar parts of
  the activation space. We want those tokens to attend to one another, even if
  they are located far apart from one another in the sequence.

  (need a picture + example to make this concrete)

1. **MHSA (attempt 3: subspace-specific similarities)**. This approach is not
very flexible. It imposes a universal notion of similarity between tokens. But
in reality, there are many types of similarity that might be useful in different
contexts. For example, ``scale'' and ''arpeggio'' might be similar in the sense
of both being musical terms, while ``scale'' and ``fish'' might be similar in
the sense of being a part of the animal. To this end, we project the tokens
$x_{n}$ onto a subspace spanned by the $M < D$ rows of $U \in \reals^{M \times
D}$. The projected coordinates are given by $Ux_{n}$, and their associated
similarity is,
  \begin{align*}
  A_{nn'} &= \frac{\exp{\left(Ux_{n}\right)^\top Ux_{n^{\prime}}}}{\sum_{n' = 1}^{N}\exp{\left(Ux_{n}\right)^\top Ux_{n^{\prime}}}}
  \end{align*}
  By using different choices of $U$, we can capture different notions of
  similarity.

1. **MHSA (attempt 4: asymmetry)**. The first way we use different $U$'s is by
breaking symmetry in the similarity measurement. Specifically, we change the formula above to
  \begin{align*}
  A_{nn'} &= \frac{\exp{\left(U_{k}x_{n}\right)^\top U_{q}x_{n^{\prime}}}}{\sum_{n' = 1}^{N}\exp{\left(U_{k}x_{n}\right)^\top U_{q}x_{n^{\prime}}}}
  \end{align*}
   In the literature, the quantities $U_{q}x$ and $U_{k}x$ are called queries
   and keys, respectively. Token $n'$ ``asks'' (query) which of the other tokens
   $n$ are similar to it, the answer depends on whether the other token's
   ``key'' matches it.

1. **MHSA** Finally, we can consider many keys and queries. This will allow us
to capture the different senses of ``scale'' above. Specifically, for each layer
$m$, we define $H$ matrices,
\begin{align*}
U_{kh} \in \reals^{M \times D} \qquad U_{qh} \in \reals^{M \times D}
\end{align*}
which allows us to create $H$ different versions of the attention matrices
$A_{h}$,
\begin{align*}
\left[A_{h}\right]_{nn'} &= \frac{\left(U_{kh}x_{n}\right)^\top\left(U_{qh}x_{n'}\right)}{\sum_{n' = 1}^{N}\left(U_{kh}x_{n}\right)^\top\left(U_{qh}x_{n'}\right)}
\end{align*}
Then, instead of simply using
\begin{align*}
Y^{m} = X^{m - 1}A^{m}
\end{align*}
like we defined above, we consider
\begin{align*}
Y^{m} = \sum_{h = 1}^{H} V_{h}^{m} X^{m - 1}A_{h}^{m}
\end{align*}
where $V_{h}^{m} \in \reals^{D \times D}$ is a linear transformation of the
attention-mixed input that gives some additional flexibility. In the literature
$V_{h}$ are called ``values'' -- when the query matches a key according to the
similarities $A_{h}$, we return the values $V_{h}^{m}X^{m - 1}$.

1. To summarize, we have parameters $U_{qh}^{m}, U_{kh}^{m}, V_{h}^{m}$ for each
layer $m$ which learn the similarities between pairs of tokens and linearly
transforms the representations $X^{m - 1}$ in layer $m - 1$ into $X^{m}$ at
layer $m$.

## Stage 2: MLP across features

1. The second stage of the transformer applies a nonlinear transformation to
each of the $N$ tokens one at a time. This stage is responsible for merging
information across the $D$ features at each token. Specifically, write the
$n^{th}$ tokens of $X^{m}$ and $Y^{m} as $x_{n}^{m}$ and $y_{n}^{m}$,
respectively. Then, we process each token one at a time using an
\begin{align*}
x_{n}^{m} &= y_{n}^{m} + \text{MLP}\left(y_{n}^{m}\right)
\end{align*}

1. The pattern above
$x_{\text{out}} \leftarrow x_{\text{in}} + f(x_{\text{in}})$ is an example of a
``residual connection.'' The idea is that it's easier to learn a modification of
the input than an entirely new transformation of the input $x_{\text{out}} =
f(x_{\text{in}})$. At the start of training, the transformation is just the
identity, and over the course of training we gradually modify it.

(add a picture)

1. Another trick to streamline training is to normalize the inputs before both
the MHSA and the MLP steps. It's common to use LayerNorm, introduced in the
previous note, across tokens (rather than samples). Putting everything together,
we arrive at the final transformer block.

\begin{align*}
x_{n}^{m} &= y_{n}^{m} + \text{MLP}\left(\bar{y}_{n}^{m}\right)\\
\bar{Y}^{m} &= \text{LayerNorm}\left(Y^{m}\right)\\
Y^{m} &= X^{m - 1} + \text{MHSA}\left(\bar{X}^{m - 1}\right)\\
\bar{X}^{m - 1} &= \text{LayerNorm}\left(X^{m - 1}\right)
\end{align*}

## Code Example
